---
title: "Equivalence with tna: synthetic wide alphabet"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>",
  message = FALSE, warning = FALSE,
  fig.width = 10, fig.height = 7, dpi = 150
)
source("_equivalence-helpers.R")
```

30 simulated sessions (length 15--25) with a wider, asymmetric alphabet:
5 Human codes (`Hask`, `Hexplain`, `Hcorrect`, `Hverify`, `Hreflect`)
and 4 AI codes (`Aplan`, `Aexec`, `Adebug`, `Aexplain`). Transitions
are drawn from a random row-normalised matrix, so the graph is dense
and every edge gets visited multiple times. This stresses larger
transition matrices and a non-square actor split.

The pipeline below runs the same htna ↔ tna ↔ Nestimate three-way
equivalence checks as the
[main equivalence article](tna-equivalence.html).

## Setup

```{r}
library(tna)
library(htna)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)
library(tibble)

ds <- ds_synthetic_wide()
```

```{r child = "_equivalence-body.Rmd"}
```
